nodes.description: Imperforate anus or other anorectal anomalies can occur in the syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Phenotypic spectrum associated with SPECC1L pathogenic variants: new families and critical review of the nosology of Teebi, Opitz GBBB, and Baraitser-Winter syndromes.
evidence.snippet: Congenital anomalies include hypospadias, cleft lip/palate, laryngeal and tracheoesophageal abnormalities (typically: cleft larynx), imperforate anus and cardiac defects.

nodes.description: Widely spaced eyes are one of the main clinical signs.

evidence.reference_title: SPECC1L Mutations Are Not Common in Sporadic Cases of Opitz G/BBB Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: The main clinical signs are represented by hypertelorism, laryngo-tracheo-esophageal defects and hypospadias.

nodes.description: Hypospadias is a major urogenital malformation in affected males.

evidence.reference_title: A Rare Case of Telecanthus-Hypospadias Syndrome in a Pediatric Patient.
evidence.snippet: Hypertelorism and hypospadias are the main characteristics of telecanthus-hypospadias syndrome

nodes.description: Laryngeal cleft is a representative laryngo-tracheo-esophageal malformation.

evidence.reference_title: A Rare Case of Telecanthus-Hypospadias Syndrome in a Pediatric Patient.
evidence.snippet: it can also include other midline structural anomalies, such as cleft lip and palate, cryptorchidism, congenital heart problem, laryngotracheal cleft, esophageal fistula, and irregular scrotum.

evidence.reference_title: First trimester ultrasound features of X-linked Opitz syndrome and early molecular diagnosis: case report and review of the literature.
evidence.snippet: Pathogenetic variants in MID1 gene have been reported in individuals with XLOS.

evidence.reference_title: Opitz syndrome: improving clinical interpretation of intronic variants in MID1 gene.
evidence.snippet: Minigene assay supports functional effects from MID1 intronic variants.

nodes.description: Human iPSC-derived organoid modeling shows that loss of RING-domain containing MID1 isoforms causes early patterning defects, a neurogenic deficit, reduced neural tissue, and increased choroid plexus-like tissue, supporting a developmental mechanism for CNS and neurodevelopmental involvement.
nodes.id: MID1 RING-domain isoform loss causes brain-patterning defects

evidence.reference_title: Absence of the RING domain in MID1 results in patterning defects in the developing human brain.
evidence.snippet: By using genome-edited human induced pluripotent stem cell lines, we here show that absence of isoforms containing the RING domain of MID1 causes severe patterning defects in human brain organoids.

evidence.reference_title: Absence of the RING domain in MID1 results in patterning defects in the developing human brain.
evidence.snippet: We observed a prominent neurogenic deficit with a reduction in neural tissue and a concomitant increase in choroid plexus-like structures.

nodes.description: Pathogenic MID1 variation alters an X-linked microtubule-associated ubiquitin-ligase pathway. The resulting defects in cytoskeletal signaling, protein ubiquitination, and embryonic patterning help explain the congenital midline malformations in craniofacial, foregut-airway, urogenital, anorectal, cardiac, and nervous-system structures.
nodes.id: MID1 loss of function disrupts midline developmental patterning

evidence.reference_title: Opitz syndrome: improving clinical interpretation of intronic variants in MID1 gene.
evidence.snippet: Loss-of-function variants in MID1 are the most common cause of Opitz G/BBB syndrome (OS).

evidence.reference_title: Phenotypic spectrum associated with SPECC1L pathogenic variants: new families and critical review of the nosology of Teebi, Opitz GBBB, and Baraitser-Winter syndromes.
evidence.snippet: MID1 is an RBCC (RING-finger, B-boxes and Coiled-coil) scaffold protein. It forms a large microtubule-associated protein complex that stabilizes microtubules.

nodes.description: Affected individuals require coordinated supportive management across pediatrics, urology or pediatric surgery, cardiology, craniofacial or oral surgery, and other specialties depending on organ involvement.

evidence.reference_title: A Rare Case of Telecanthus-Hypospadias Syndrome in a Pediatric Patient.
evidence.snippet: A multidisciplinary approach involved pediatricians, oral surgeons, cardiologists, and pediatric surgeons.

nodes.description: Orofacial clefting is part of the midline malformation spectrum.

evidence.reference_title: A Rare Case of Telecanthus-Hypospadias Syndrome in a Pediatric Patient.
evidence.snippet: it can also include other midline structural anomalies, such as cleft lip and palate, cryptorchidism, congenital heart problem, laryngotracheal cleft, esophageal fistula, and irregular scrotum.

nodes.description: Management is primarily anomaly-directed, including staged hypospadias repair and repair of clefting or other structural defects when present.

evidence.reference_title: A Rare Case of Telecanthus-Hypospadias Syndrome in a Pediatric Patient.
evidence.snippet: The patient underwent surgery for first-stage hypospadias correction and was advised to follow up for additional surgery and maintenance procedures before being discharged.

nodes.description: Structural cardiac anomalies can include ventricular septal defect.

evidence.reference_title: A Rare Case of Telecanthus-Hypospadias Syndrome in a Pediatric Patient.
evidence.snippet: He had hypertelorism, hypospadias, a ventricular septal defect, and a history of cryptorchidism.
